main_sections.title: Winds of Change
main_sections.prose_summary: This informational chapter traces the cultural history and regional diversity of the Indian hand fan, known as the pankha or punkha. The word originates from 'pankh', meaning a bird's feather, while the term pankhi refers to a small, plumed fan used in ancient India — evidence of which appears in Buddhist wall paintings at Ajanta dating to the 2nd century CE. Pankhas were used in temples to fan deities and in royal courts to fan kings, and they ranged from tiny two-inch pieces to large fans requiring a person's full arm strength. Over time, they became significant cultural goods traded across routes and were considered exotic and stylish. Though pankhas shared common usage across India, each region developed distinct varieties defined by local materials and intricate designs: Rajasthan produces the appliqué, zardozi, temple brass, and painted cardboard pankhas; Gujarat is known for mirror-work cotton, beaded silver-handled, and hand-stitched leather (Kutch) fans; Bengal crafts delicate fans from the sola plant's milky-white centre (Tal Patar Pankha); Uttar Pradesh's Phadh fans are adorned with gold, silver zari, silk and satin; Bihar's fans are colourful and sturdy bamboo; and Indian tribal communities use grass, metal, cane, palm leaf, silk, and brass with geometrical patterns and white-ink-on-red designs. In modern times pankhas have shifted from functional to decorative objects, now serving as traditional craft items and a commercial livelihood for artisans. The chapter argues that celebrating pankha culture through workshops and exhibitions is the key strategy for preserving the craft, giving contemporary makers a platform to demonstrate their skill and earn a sustainable livelihood. The chapter is accompanied by an abridged retelling of O. Henry's 'The Last Leaf' as a 'read and enjoy' enrichment story under Learning Beyond the Text, which explores themes of art, friendship, and the will to live through the characters of Sue, Johnsy, and Behrman.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Does anyone in your family use a hand fan? What do you call it in your language? What is it made of?

Work in pairs. Study the pictures given below carefully and complete the information about fans from Assam, Kerala, and Uttar Pradesh (shape and material). Discuss your answers with your classmates and teacher.

Material of Assam fan
Shape of Kerala fan
Shape of Uttar Pradesh fan

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Match the underlined words in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher. (Words: indigenous, innovative, industrious, intricate, invoked, initiatives)

innovative
industrious
intricate
invoked

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs to complete the table on pankha (State, Type of Fan, Material Used). Share your answers with your classmates and teacher. States covered: Rajasthan (appliqué hand fan, zardozi hand fan, temple hand fans), Gujarat (mirror work hand fans, beads hand fan, leather hand fan from Kutch), West Bengal (sola hand fans), Uttar Pradesh, Bihar.

question_bank.stem: Material of Rajasthan appliqué hand fan
Type of Rajasthan fan made with gold thread
Material of Rajasthan temple hand fans
Material of Gujarat mirror work hand fans
Type of Gujarat fan made with beads
Type of Gujarat fan made with leather decorated with thread and wool
Material of West Bengal sola hand fans
Type of Uttar Pradesh fan made with pure gold, silver zari, silk and satin frills
Type of Bihar fan made with bamboo

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. (Extract 1: 'Over time, pankhas became significant cultural goods...' Extract 2: 'With time and the advent of technology...')

question_bank.stem: Extract 1(i): State whether the following sentence is true or false. 'Pankhas were one of the most popular items of commerce.'
Extract 1(ii): Why has the word 'traditional' been used to describe pankhas?
Extract 1(iii): Complete the following statement. 'The sentence They were considered exotic and stylish is an opinion and not a fact because ______.'
Extract 1(iv): Infer one reason for commonality in the use of pankhas across India.

Extract 1(v): Select the correct assertion for the given reason. Reason: Pankhas were made of indigenous materials, unique to the region, with elaborate designs. A. Each kind of pankha could be distinguished from the other. B. Pankhas were used by many people.
Extract 2(i): Infer one negative impact of technological advancement on pankha.
Extract 2(ii): Complete the statement. The writer refers to 'pankhas' not just as an object but as a 'culture' because ______.
Extract 2(iii): Select a line from the extract which depicts how the role of the pankha has changed over the years.
Extract 2(iv): List one way in which the increase in demand of pankhas might benefit artisans.
Extract 2(v): Select the factor that has contributed to the commercialisation of pankhas. A. cultural preservation B. economic demand C. technological advancements D. artisan initiative

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions. 1. How does the title 'Winds of Change' capture the essence of the chapter? 2. Support the following statement with any two relevant examples: 'The structure and design of pankhas are testimony to the cultural identity of the region.' 3. Evaluate how the balance between preserving traditional craftsmanship and incorporating innovative designs will help pankhas retain their presence. 4. How might pankha-making workshops contribute to preservation of this traditional craft? 5. Assess how celebrating pankhas could be beneficial to artisans and the craft. 6. How does the restriction of pankha to decorative purposes reflect the changing cultural role of traditional fans in modern India?

question_bank.stem: How does the title 'Winds of Change' capture the essence of the chapter?
Support: 'The structure and design of pankhas are testimony to the cultural identity of the region' with two examples.
Evaluate how the balance between preserving traditional craftsmanship and incorporating innovative designs will help pankhas.
How might pankha-making workshops contribute to preservation?
Assess how celebrating pankhas could be beneficial to artisans and the craft.
How does the restriction to decorative purposes reflect the changing cultural role of pankhas in modern India?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Find the word pairs for the following fixed expressions and write their meanings by selecting from the box. (Words: high/tear, cut/fast, fact/sundry, all/thin [given: all and sundry = everyone], wear/figures, time/run, thick/again, hard/dry). Then match the following meanings: (i) in a difficult situation without help or money (ii) the damage to an object due to normal use (iii) accurate and detailed information (iv) even when there are problems or difficulties (v) everyone, not just a few special people (vi) often; on many or all occasions (vii) to make a quick or sudden escape.

Fixed expression for cut + fast
Fixed expression for fact + sundry
Fixed expression for wear + figures
Fixed expression for time + run
Fixed expression for thick + again
Fixed expression for hard + dry

(iii) My scooter (dashed against/ran into) a car. (iv) I must (take/own) responsibility for my success. (v) I would like to (tone up/improve) my grammar.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following sentences, underline the verbs (have become, have worked, have adopted, has transformed — present perfect tense). Now fill in the blanks with the present perfect form of verbs in the following paragraph about puppets: (i) create (ii) master (iii) evolve (iv) pass (v) perform.

question_bank.stem: Puppeteers (i) ______ (create) intricate characters.
They (ii) ______ (master) the delicate movements.
Puppetry (iii) ______ (evolve), using modern technology.
Many puppeteers (iv) ______ (pass) down their craft through generations.
They (v) ______ (perform) in theatres, on television, and at festivals.

listening.transcript_text: Rohan and Priya discuss choosing a pankha as a birthday gift for their grandmother. Rohan suggests a traditional pankha since Grandma loves traditional crafts, and recalls she enjoys sitting in the verandah in the evenings. Priya wonders if Grandma would prefer something heavier and sturdier, like the brass fans they saw at a craft fair. Rohan agrees she mentioned liking items with a bit of weight but worries a heavy one would be hard to move around. Priya suggests something in between — not too heavy, not too light — and pretty enough for Grandma to show her friends. Rohan adds it should be small enough to keep under her pillow when not in use, as Grandma likes keeping special things close. Priya suggests the appliqué embroidered pankhas from Rajasthan, which their mother says Grandma loves. They narrow the options to two: a lightweight bamboo pankha with delicate beadwork, or a medium-weight embroidered pankha with mirror work. They decide to consult their mother, who knows Grandma's tastes best. Rohan suggests also customising it with Grandma's initials. They agree and go to ask their mother together.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a dialogue between a boy and a girl discussing which type of pankha they should choose as a gift. As you listen, choose four statements out of seven that are true from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: 1. Rohan thinks Grandma prefers heavier items because they feel more substantial.
2. Priya suggests getting a pankha that can be easily moved around.
3. Rohan believes Grandma enjoys sitting in the verandah during the evenings.
4. Priya knows Grandma likes to keep special items close to her.
5. Rohan thinks a pankha with intricate designs would be too delicate for Grandma's liking.
6. Priya suggests choosing between a bamboo pankha with beadwork or an embroidered pankha with mirror work.
7. Rohan decides they should buy the pankha without consulting their mom.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in groups of four. Look at the pictures of four different kinds of fans (hand fan, ceiling fan, table fan, handheld fan). Each of you in the group will pick one kind of fan. Imagine yourself to be that fan. Use the given points: Introduce yourself as the fan you have chosen. Describe yourself (your features, etc.). What do you like about yourself? What do you feel is your weakness? What makes you unique?

Describe an artefact you have made in your craft class or learnt from someone. Begin with collection and organisation of facts with the help of the pointers given. Do not include your opinion in the factual description.

Identify any art or craft item from your region and make a presentation describing its features, materials, and process involved in making it, its usage, etc. Use points: (I) Name of the object and its physical characteristics—appearance, shape, size, colour, etc.; What is it made up of? Which region does it belong to? How has it been used traditionally? Is there an origin story associated with it? How has it evolved over time? How can it be used effectively and safely in today's time?

You can also make your own hand fan. Take ideas from the pictures given and in groups of four, make your own hand fan. Display the hand fans in the class and describe the process used to make it. Also, read and enjoy the story 'The Last Leaf' by O. Henry (abridged) — about two young artists Sue and Johnsy, and their neighbour Behrman, whose masterpiece painted leaf saves Johnsy's will to live.
